title: Refund Rate & Returns Analysis
business_definition: Refund rate = refund amount or count / gross order amount or count. High refund rates signal product quality issues, expectation mismatches, fraud, or fulfillment problems. Refunds directly reduce net revenue.
diagnostic_questions: Is the refund rate spike concentrated in a specific product category, SKU, or supplier?
Did a specific batch, fulfillment center, or shipping partner cause the issue?
Is this voluntary (customer request) or involuntary (failed charge, chargeback)?
Are refunds being processed within policy windows or are late refunds increasing liability?

causal_relationships.symptom: refund rate spiked in a specific period
causal_relationships.detection_sql: SELECT refund_reason, product_category, COUNT(*) AS refunds, SUM(refund_amount) AS total_refunded, COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER () AS pct FROM refunds WHERE refunded_at >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC

SELECT payment_method, COUNT(CASE WHEN is_chargeback THEN 1 END) AS chargebacks, COUNT(*) AS total_orders, COUNT(CASE WHEN is_chargeback THEN 1 END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS chargeback_rate FROM orders GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 4 DESC

inflation_causes.cause: Test refunds or internal credits included in refund figures
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT refund_type, COUNT(*), SUM(refund_amount) FROM refunds GROUP BY 1

deflation_causes.cause: Store credits given instead of cash refunds not counted
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT resolution_type, COUNT(*), SUM(amount) FROM customer_complaints WHERE resolved_at >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' GROUP BY 1

anti_patterns: Do not measure refund rate only by count — a few high-value refunds matter more than many small ones
Do not compare refund rates across product categories without accounting for expected return rate differences

sql_assets.refund_rate_by_category: SELECT product_category, COUNT(r.refund_id) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(o.order_id), 0) AS refund_rate, SUM(r.refund_amount) AS total_refunded FROM orders o LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.order_id = r.order_id WHERE o.order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
